es.example: Tuve que ir a la fuerza.
es.exampleTranslation: I had to go whether I liked it or not.
es.regionNote: Mexico also says 'a fuerzas' (colloquial). Reviewer to confirm register.

it.exampleTranslation: I have to go, no way around it.

bridge.note: Force wears different prepositions: per forza vs a la fuerza. And the Italian moonlights as an interjection — Per forza! = 'well, obviously!' — a use the Spanish phrase never developed.

interference.es_to_it: Spanish A LA pulls 'alla forza' — Italian compresses: per forza, bare. Also Italian 'Per forza!' alone means 'obviously!' — a discourse job the Spanish doesn't do.
interference.it_to_es: Italian bare form pulls 'por fuerza' — dated in most of the Spanish-speaking world; say a la fuerza (or, MX colloquial, a fuerzas).

review.notes: From Joe's Italian teacher's prepositions crash course, 2026-07-16. ES pairing chosen by Claude, unverified — reviewer to confirm.
